title: skopeo sync with `--preserve-digest` does not behave as expected

comments.body:
Hello , @holgpar just to make sure , does the digest that you have provided in the yml file correspond to the manifest index digest or a digest for a specifc image manifest , because the expected result will depend on that.

If it is a digest for a specific image manifest , from my previous experiences the --override-arch flag just gets ignored if i change the architecture using the --override-arch. 
Let's consider this sync.yaml file:
```
 docker.io:
  images:
    alpine:
      - sha256:806ee0e00ae6daa436f7385d1ea4356b38a71ed1f136ac428925326bb8b53abe
```
 This digest correspond to the amd64 build for the alpine:3.21.4 image  , if i run this command (here the destination is a local dir (alpine/) but i believe it should be the same with a docker destination)
`bin/skopeo sync --override-arch s390x --src yaml --dest dir --scoped --preserve-digests --digestfile digest.txt sync.yaml alpine/`
As you can see i tried to override the arch to be an s390x , maybe you would expect the command to fail , or at least that the digest will change , but this is not the case.
```
cat digest.txt:

sha256:806ee0e00ae6daa436f7385d1ea4356b38a71ed1f136ac428925326bb8b53abe dir:../../test_man/alpine/docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:806ee0e00ae6daa436f7385d1ea4356b38a71ed1f136ac428925326bb8b53abe
```
as you can see the --override-arch was the one being ignored , so the --preserve-digest won't cause any issues. (You can also see the contents of the alpine directory and inspect the manifest file to make sure).

Now let's change the sync.yaml file:

```
docker.io:
  images:
    alpine:
      - sha256:b6a6be0ff92ab6db8acd94f5d1b7a6c2f0f5d10ce3c24af348d333ac6da80685 
```
This time the digest represents the index manifest digest of the alpine:3.21.4 image , if i rerun the same command:
 `bin/skopeo sync --override-arch s390x --src yaml --dest dir --scoped --preserve-digests --digestfile digest1.txt sync.yaml alpine/`
I will get this output :
```
cat digest1.txt

sha256:a87c3ccc65611ee9409fb74e348b372069b86a2e8fc0a919183fdec2d568ec8b dir:../../test_man/alpine/docker.io/library/alpine@sha256:b6a6be0ff92ab6db8acd94f5d1b7a6c2f0f5d10ce3c24af348d333ac6da80685
```

You might be wondering what is this digest , it actually coressponds to the digest of the s390x build for the alpine:3.21.4 image , so this time the --override-arch actually worked.
So why didn't the preserver-digests flag fail the command ? honestly i found that this is the expected behaviour and the intuitive one , since the image manifest index actually have the reference of all the different builds of that specific image , it doesn't represent an image itself (more precisely an image manifest) , so i would assume that skopeo will be able to recognize that and resolve it to a specific image manifest for a specific build (This is just my understanding , i didn't read the source code of the sync command yet , i might be wrong , let's wait for a maintainer to confirm or deny what i said).

@mtrmac maybe you can provide a more accurate explanation than mine.

Hello again @holgpar , sorry man for the late reply.
The digest isn't being preserved , because you are syncing the image , the amd64/linux variant of the haproxy image , so you will get the digest of that image (more specifically the image's manifest), you can't get the digest of the manifest index , no matter which architecture you choose , or even if you omit the --override-arch/--override-os , you will never get the digest of the manifest index as a result , instead you will get the digest of the manifest of the synced container image.

Hi @GGh41th , yes, it is clear that there are situations when the digest cannot be preserved. My question is rather about the behavior of the `--preserve-digests` option. Because from the documentation, I would expect that the command fails if

- the `--preserve-digests` is set
- the digests cannot be preserved

However, in my examples, the command actually succeeds. Sorry that my question was not formulated more clearly. I will do a minor edit.

Thanks for your report.

The documentation of `--preserve-digests` says:
> This option does not change what will be copied; consider using `--all` at the same time.

i.e., in this case, the amd64 variant was copied, and _that_ digest (`sha256:8a304a313c511a003342fc32f1afc40f211ad4c7f1f90a1decbc3d365ceb3125`) was preserved correctly.

The design where `--preserve-digests` does _not_ imply `--all` was, originally, necessary for backward compatibility — but, at that time, I think only sources defined by a tag were considered: `--preserve-digests` without `all` of a tagged multi-platform image would copy one per-platform image, without modifying the digest _of that platform_.

Correspondingly, when copying a multi-platform image specified by digest, we _also_ copy only one per-platform image, without modifying that one.

So, overall, I think using `--preserve-digests --all` is the way to obtain the desired behavior.

I fully agree it is confusing, but, also, I’m not sure we can change that any more.

We might add a warning when using `--preserve-digests` on a multi-platform image specified by a digest, but then, we would need to add another option to silence that warning… I’m not sure that’s worth it.
